topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The string quartet swelled, a delicate, almost mocking melody against the backdrop of the opulent ballroom. I was nursing a lukewarm champagne flute, trying to appear engaged in a conversation about floral arrangements, when a familiar silhouette caught my eye near the entrance. It was him. Mark. He was standing next to Sarah, laughing, a carefree ease radiating from him that felt entirely alien to my current state. My breath hitched, a small, involuntary catch in my chest. I gripped my glass so tightly my knuckles turned white. Every nerve ending seemed to be screaming, and I focused intensely on the pattern of the crystal on the table, willing myself to remain still, to not let the sudden, sharp jolt of recognition pull me out of my composure. When he finally turned, our eyes met across the crowded room. The easy smile vanished, replaced by a flicker of something guarded, and the air around me suddenly felt thin. I mumbled something about needing air and practically fled toward the terrace doors, the sound of the music now just a distant, muffled roar.

[story 2]

I was attempting to blend into the periphery of the reception, a strategy that usually worked wonders for managing social anxiety. The noise level was intense—laughter, clinking glasses, the pervasive hum of celebration—but all I could register was the sudden, jarring appearance of Alex. He was across the room, holding court near the bar, looking effortlessly charming. My internal monologue stalled. My hands, which had been loosely clasped in front of me, began to tremble almost imperceptibly. I felt a strange pressure building behind my eyes, a tightness in my throat that made swallowing difficult. I watched him for a long moment, cataloging every subtle shift in his posture, before a wave of nausea washed over me. I excused myself abruptly, claiming an urgent need to check on something downstairs, and navigated the crowded floor with a jerky, unnatural gait, desperate to find a quiet corner where I could simply disappear.

[story 3]

The warmth of the room felt suffocating. I was standing near the buffet, pretending to examine a platter of canapés, trying to anchor myself to the mundane details of the event. Then, he walked into my line of sight. It was Liam. He looked exactly the same—the familiar curve of his jaw, the way he held his shoulders—but the context had shifted entirely, and suddenly, everything felt exposed. A coldness seeped into my limbs, making my skin prickle. I stopped breathing for a second, the surrounding chatter dissolving into a dull, meaningless buzz. My gaze locked onto his, and the world narrowed down to the space between us. A frantic urge to bolt seized me. I didn't wait for an invitation or a cue; I simply turned and walked away, the sound of my own hurried footsteps echoing too loudly in the sudden silence of my own mind.
